explanation: “Cappello” (hat) needs the double p. With one p, “capello” is a single hair — the doppie (double consonants) change the word outright. Hold the sound: cap-PEL-lo.
“Bellissimo” (very beautiful) doubles BOTH the l and the s. Dropping either (belissimo, bellisimo) is the commonest superlative slip.
“Penna” (pen) has a double n. “Pena”, with one n, means pain or sorrow — a true minimal pair: the doppia is the only difference.
“Sette” (seven) doubles the t. “Sete”, single t, means thirst. Say the long tt to keep them apart.
“Nonno” (grandfather) has a double n; “nono”, single n, means ninth. The doppia carries the meaning.
“Cane” (dog) is spelt with ONE n. “Canne”, double n, means reeds or pipes — here the trap is adding a doppia that doesn’t belong.
“Mamma” (mum) doubles the m. One m (mama) is the typical under-doubling.
“Donna” (woman) has a double n. “Dona”, single n, is a real verb (he/she gives) — the doppia separates them.
“Gatto” (cat) doubles the t. “Gato” is the under-doubled error.
“Freddo” (cold) has a double d; a single d (fredo) is the slip.
“Cavallo” (horse) doubles the l. One l (cavalo) drops the doppia.
“Professore” has a double s. English ‘professor’ (one s) is the interference here.
“Bocca” (mouth) doubles the c. “Boca” is the under-doubled error.
“Fratello” (brother) has a double l; sorella (sister) likewise doubles its l.
“Sorella” (sister) doubles the l. One l (sorela) is the slip.
“Pizza” doubles the z. Italian z is often geminate; one z (piza) sounds wrong to a native ear.
“Terra” (earth) has a double r; a single r (tera) is the error.
“Birra” (beer) doubles the r. English ‘beer’ has none, so learners drop it.
“Tutto” (all) doubles the t. One t (tuto) is the under-doubling.
“Ragazzo” (boy) has a double z; ragazza (girl) doubles it too.
“Dopo” (after) has just ONE p. “Doppo” over-doubles it — a spurious doppia, the opposite error to under-doubling.
“Sabato” (Saturday) has a single b. “Sabbato” wrongly doubles it — tempting because so many Italian words do double.
“Camera” (room) has ONE m. “Cammera” adds a doppia that isn’t there.
“Capitale” (capital) keeps a single p; “cappitale” is a spurious doubling.
“Mattina” (morning) doubles the t. One t (matina) is the slip.
“Babbo” (dad) doubles the b. One b (babo) is not a word.
“Oggi” (today) doubles the g. One g (ogi) drops the doppia.
“Leggere” (to read) has a double g; a single g (legere) is the error.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “coffee (spelling of the consonants)”:
explanation: “Caffè” (coffee) doubles the f. (The final accent is a separate matter — here focus on the ff.)
“Femmina” (female) doubles the m. One m (femina) is the under-doubling.
“Un’amica”: before a feminine noun starting with a vowel, “una” elides to “un’” with an apostrophe. “Una amica” leaves it unelided; “un amica” drops the mark that feminine gender needs.
“Un amico”: the masculine “un” is already the short form — nothing is elided, so NO apostrophe. “Un’amico” is the classic over-apostrophe; “uno” is only used before z/s+cons.
“Un’ora” (an hour): ora is feminine and vowel-initial, so una → un’. Without the apostrophe (un ora) the feminine is lost.
“Un uomo” (a man): masculine, so no elision and no apostrophe. “Un’uomo” wrongly borrows the feminine mark.
“Un’idea” (an idea): idea is feminine, so una → un’. The apostrophe is obligatory.
“Un albero” (a tree): albero is masculine, so plain un with no apostrophe.
“Un’isola” (an island): isola is feminine and vowel-initial — una → un’.
“L’acqua”: the article la elides to l’ before a vowel. “La acqua” leaves the vowels colliding.
“L’uomo”: lo elides to l’ before a vowel. “Lo uomo” is unelided.
“L’amica”: la elides before a vowel — note l’ alone no longer shows gender, which is fine for the definite article.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “I agree (‘I am in agreement’)”:
explanation: “D’accordo”: di elides to d’ before the vowel of accordo. “Di accordo” is unelided.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “What is your name? — ‘what is…’”:
explanation: “Qual è” takes NO apostrophe: qual is a truncated form (troncamento), not an elision, so nothing is dropped. “Qual’è” is the single most common native spelling error.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “a little (‘a bit of…’)”:
explanation: “Un po’”: po’ is poco with its end cut off, marked by an apostrophe. “Un po” forgets the mark; “un pò” wrongly uses an accent where an apostrophe belongs.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “the Italian language”:
explanation: “L’italiano” (the Italian language): lo elides to l’ before the vowel.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “at one o’clock”:
explanation: “All’una”: a+la = alla, which elides to all’ before una’s vowel.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “some water (‘of the water’)”:
explanation: “Dell’acqua”: di+la = della, elided to dell’ before acqua.
“Sant’Antonio”: before a vowel, Santo elides to Sant’. San is used before consonants (San Marco); Santo unelided is wrong here.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “me too (‘also I’)”:
explanation: “Anch’io”: anche elides to anch’ before io. Both forms are heard, but the elided one is standard in writing.
“Un esame” (an exam): esame is masculine, so no apostrophe — even though it starts with a vowel.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “half an hour”:
explanation: “Mezz’ora”: mezza elides to mezz’ before ora.
“Un’università”: università is feminine, so una → un’. (The final accent is separate from the apostrophe.)
“Senz’altro”: senza elides to senz’ before altro — a fixed expression meaning ‘certainly’.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “that man (‘that’ + man)”:
explanation: “Quell’uomo”: quello elides to quell’ before a vowel, like the article lo.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “where is…? (‘where is’)”:
explanation: “Dov’è” (where is): dove elides to dov’ before è. The apostrophe replaces the dropped -e.
“Amici”: amico is the famous exception — its plural stays SOFT (amici), with no h. “Amichi” wrongly hardens it.

markpoints.label: amiche — h keeps the hard c

explanation: “Amiche”: amica keeps its hard c in the plural, so an h is inserted — amiche. “Amice” would sound ‘a-mee-cheh’.

markpoints.label: lunghe — h keeps the hard g

explanation: “Lunghe”: lunga keeps its hard g, so the plural inserts an h — lunghe. “Lunge” softens it; “lungie” invents a stray i.

markpoints.label: lunghi — h keeps the hard g

explanation: “Lunghi”: lungo → lunghi, the h preserving the hard g. “Lungi” exists only as an archaic adverb (‘far’), not this plural.

markpoints.label: giochi — h keeps the hard c

explanation: “Giochi”: gioco keeps the hard c, so h is added — giochi.

markpoints.label: laghi — h keeps the hard g

explanation: “Laghi”: lago → laghi, h preserving the hard g.

markpoints.label: alberghi — h keeps the hard g

explanation: “Alberghi”: albergo → alberghi with the hard-g h.

markpoints.label: tedeschi — h keeps the hard sc

explanation: “Tedeschi”: tedesco keeps its hard -sco sound, written -schi.

markpoints.label: fuochi — h keeps the hard c

explanation: “Fuochi”: fuoco → fuochi, h keeping the hard c.

markpoints.label: banche — h keeps the hard c

explanation: “Banche”: banca → banche, h preserving the hard c.
“Medici”: like amici, medico softens in the plural (stress pulls it) — no h. “Medichi” over-hardens.
“Biologi”: nouns in -logo make -logi (soft), not -loghi. “Biologhi” is the over-correction.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “you look for” (tu, from cercare):
explanation: “Cerchi”: verbs in -care add an h before endings in -e/-i to keep the hard c — cerc + h + i. “Cerci” softens it; “cerchii” doubles the i.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “you pay” (tu, from pagare):
explanation: “Paghi”: verbs in -gare add an h before -e/-i — pag + h + i — to keep the hard g.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “I will look for” (from cercare):
explanation: “Cercherò”: the -care h appears before the future’s -erò too. Without it (cercerò) the c would go soft.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “you play” (tu, from giocare):
explanation: “Giochi”: giocare keeps the hard c with an h before -i.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “you explain” (tu, from spiegare):
explanation: “Spieghi”: spiegare adds the h before -i to hold the hard g.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “we pay” (noi, from pagare):
explanation: “Paghiamo”: the -gare h also appears before the -iamo ending.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “we eat” (noi, from mangiare):
explanation: “Mangiamo”: -giare verbs are ALREADY soft, so they add NO h — contrast paghiamo (-gare, needs h) with mangiamo (-giare, none). “Manghiamo” over-applies the rule.
“Arance”: in -cia after a CONSONANT (aran-cia), the plural drops the i → arance. “Arancie” keeps a redundant i.
“Camicie”: in -cia after a VOWEL (cami-cia), the plural keeps the i → camicie. “Camice” is a different word (a lab coat).
“Cielo” (sky) keeps a silent i after the c. “Celo”, without it, is the verb ‘I hide’ — the i is doing lexical work here.
“Scienza” (science) keeps the i in -scie-. “Scenza” drops it. (Coscienza behaves the same way.)
“Efficiente” keeps the i (-ciente). “Efficente” is an extremely common native slip. Sufficiente works the same way.
“Acqua” (water) belongs to the small cqu family: a hard c THEN qu. “Aqua” drops the c; “acua” drops the q.
“Scuola” (school) is spelt with cu, not qu — sc-u-o-la. “Squola” hears the /kw/ and reaches wrongly for qu.
“Cuore” (heart) uses cu. “Quore” is the classic qu-overreach.
“Quando” (when) uses qu, the normal spelling of the /kw/ sound.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “how much / how many”:
explanation: “Quanto” (how much) takes qu.
“Cinque” (five): the /kwe/ is written que. “Cinqe” forgets the u.
“Quattro” (four): qu for the /kw/, plus a double t. “Cuattro” mis-spells the /kw/; “quatro” drops the doppia.
“Acquisto” (a purchase) is in the cqu family, like acqua. “Aquisto” drops the c.
“Quaderno” (notebook) takes qu.
“Quale” (which) uses qu.
“Qui” (here) uses qu. “Cui” is a different word (whom/which), spelt with cu — a true meaning contrast.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “was born (he/she — passato remoto)”:
explanation: “Nacque” (was born): the passato remoto of nascere joins the cqu family (also piacque, tacque). “Naque” drops the c.
“Aquila” (eagle) is plain qu — NOT cqu. “Acquila” over-applies the acqua pattern; only a short list of words takes cqu.
“Innocuo” (harmless) uses cu, not qu — the u belongs to a separate syllable (in-no-cu-o).
“Quindici” (fifteen) takes qu.
“Ho” (I have) carries a silent h to tell it from “o” (or). The h is written but not pronounced.
“Hai” (you have) needs the silent h; “ai” without it means ‘to the’.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “he / she has”:
explanation: “Ha” (he/she has) takes the silent h; “a” without it is the preposition ‘to/at’.
“Hanno” (they have) needs the h; “anno” without it means ‘year’ — a real word, so the h truly matters.
“Anno” (year) has NO h. “Hanno”, with the h, is ‘they have’ — here the trap is adding avere’s h where it doesn’t belong.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “to Rome (‘to/at Rome’)”:
explanation: “A Roma”: the preposition a has no h. “Ha” belongs only to the verb (he/she has).

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “tea or coffee (‘or’)”:
explanation: “O” (or) has no h. “Ho” with the h is ‘I have’.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “Anna is hungry” (‘has hunger’):
explanation: “Anna ha fame”: ‘to be hungry’ is avere fame, so the verb ha with its silent h. “Anna a fame” would read ‘Anna to hunger’.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “They have two children”:
explanation: “Loro hanno due figli”: the verb hanno keeps its silent h; anno would mean ‘year’.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “Have you eaten?”:
explanation: “Hai mangiato?”: the auxiliary hai carries the silent h.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “They arrived a year ago”:
explanation: “Un anno fa” (a year ago): anno the noun has no h; only the verb hanno does.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “I have a dog”:
explanation: “Ho un cane” (I have a dog): the verb ho takes the silent h.
“Famiglia” (family) spells its /ly/ sound with gli. “Familia” uses a plain l (Spanish-style); “famillia” doubles the l instead.
“Figlio” (son): the gl before i gives the /ly/ sound. “Filio” loses the g.
“Moglie” (wife): gl gives the /ly/. “Molie” drops the g.
“Bagno” (bath): gn gives the /ny/ sound. “Banio” spells it out with n+i.
“Sogno” (dream): gn = /ny/. “Sonio” misses the g.
“Montagna” (mountain): gn for the /ny/ sound.
“Cognome” (surname): gn = /ny/, even though English ‘cognomen’ keeps them apart.

prompt: Choose the correct spelling — “everyone / each one”:
explanation: “Ognuno” (everyone): gn for /ny/.
“Pesce” (fish): sc before e gives /sh/. “Pesche”, with sch, sounds /peske/ and means ‘peaches’ — the digraph carries the meaning.
“Prosciutto” (ham): sci gives /sh/. “Prociutto” drops the s of the digraph.
“Sciare” (to ski): sci = /sh/. “Siare” loses the c.
“Biglietto” (ticket): gli for /ly/, plus a double t. “Bilietto” loses the g.
“Luglio” (July): gl gives /ly/.
“Meglio” (better): gl = /ly/.
“Aglio” (garlic): gl before i = /ly/.
“Insegnante” (teacher): gn for /ny/.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “We’ll see each other on Monday”:
explanation: “Ci vediamo lunedì”: Italian writes weekdays in lower case mid-sentence. English habit capitalises them.
“in gennaio”: months are lower case in Italian.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “I speak Italian”:
explanation: “Parlo italiano”: names of languages are lower case in Italian, unlike English.

prompt: Choose the correct phrase — “an Italian book”:
explanation: “un libro italiano”: adjectives of nationality are lower case.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “I study French”:
explanation: “Studio francese”: the language is lower case.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “The Italians love coffee”:
explanation: “Gli italiani”: even as a noun (‘the Italians’), the nationality is lower case in Italian.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “We leave next Friday”:
explanation: “venerdì” stays lower case mid-sentence.
“a dicembre”: the month is lower case.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “She is American”:
explanation: “È americana”: the adjective of nationality is lower case (the sentence-initial È is capital only because it starts the sentence).

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “On Sundays I rest”:
explanation: “domenica” is lower case; La is capital only as the first word.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “Good morning, Mr Rossi”:
explanation: “signor Rossi”: titles like signor/dottore are lower case; only the surname Rossi is capitalised.

prompt: Choose the correct sentence — “The English language is not easy”:
explanation: “l’inglese”: the language name is lower case even used as a noun.
“Difficile” (difficult) ends in -ile with an i. “Difficele” swaps in an e — the unstressed vowel is easy to mishear.
“Possibile” (possible): the ending is -ibile, not -ibele.
“Medicina” (medicine) has an i in the second syllable. “Medecina” substitutes an e.
“Dicembre” (December) begins di-, not de- — the English spelling pulls learners the wrong way.
“Minuto” (minute) starts mi-. “Menuto” swaps the i for an e.
“Comprare” (to buy) has an o. “Cumprare” raises it to u — a common o/u slip.
“Finire” (to finish) starts fi-. “Fenire” substitutes an e.
“Novembre” (November) has an o in the first syllable, not a u.
